title: Level 2: Reading Inputs (Push Button)
aim: To write an Embedded C program to read the state of a push button and control an LED accordingly on the ATmega328P.
objective: Understand GPIO input configuration using DDRx, PORTx, and PINx registers, including internal pull-up resistors and debouncing techniques.
theory:
Configuring Inputs

When you want to read a digital signal on a GPIO pin, you configure it as an INPUT. In the ATmega328P, setting a bit in the DDRx register to 0 configures the corresponding pin as an input.

The PINx Register

To actually read the voltage state on an input pin (whether it is 5V or 0V), you must read the PINx (Port Input Pins) register. For example, if PD2 is set as an input, reading PIND & (1 << 2) will tell you if the pin is HIGH or LOW.

Internal Pull-up Resistors

A bare push button connected between a pin and Ground causes a problem when the button is NOT pressed: the pin is 'floating', and its voltage is unpredictable. To fix this, you enable the microcontroller's internal pull-up resistor. When the pin is an INPUT (DDRx is 0), writing a 1 to the corresponding bit in the PORTx register connects an internal 20k-50kΩ resistor to 5V (VCC). The pin will naturally read HIGH when unpressed, and LOW when pressed (connected to Ground).

Debouncing

Mechanical switches 'bounce' when pressed, meaning the metal contacts hit each other multiple times before settling. In software, this looks like multiple rapid button presses. A simple wait (delay) after the first state change helps ignore these bounces (software debouncing).

pretest.question: Which register is used to READ the current logical state of an input pin on the ATmega328P?
pretest.explanation: The PINx register contains the actual physical logic levels present on the pins.

pretest.question: How do you enable the internal pull-up resistor on pin PD2?
options: Set DDRD bit 2 to 0, then clear PORTD bit 2 to 0
Set DDRD bit 2 to 1, then set PORTD bit 2 to 1
Set DDRD bit 2 to 0, then set PORTD bit 2 to 1
Set DDRD bit 2 to 1, then clear PORTD bit 2 to 0
pretest.explanation: To enable the pull-up, set the direction to input (DDR bit = 0) and the port value to high (PORT bit = 1).

pretest.question: What is the primary purpose of the DDRx register in the ATmega328P?
options: To configure pins as input or output
pretest.explanation: DDR configures the direction of each GPIO pin.

pretest.question: Which header file provides basic I/O register definitions for AVR?
pretest.explanation: <avr/io.h> provides register definitions for all AVR peripherals.

pretest.question: What does the volatile keyword tell the C compiler?
options: May change unexpectedly, so do not optimize reads
pretest.explanation: volatile prevents the compiler from caching the variable.

pretest.question: How many GPIO pins does the ATmega328P have in total?
pretest.explanation: 23 GPIO pins across Port B, C, and D.

pretest.question: What is the maximum clock frequency of the ATmega328P?
pretest.explanation: Supports up to 20MHz, though Arduino UNO uses 16MHz.

pretest.question: What is the purpose of sei()?
pretest.explanation: sei() sets the global interrupt enable flag in SREG.

pretest.question: Which AVR memory type retains data after power-off?
What is the width of each ATmega328P port register?
pretest.explanation: The ATmega328P is an 8-bit MCU; all port registers are 8 bits wide.

procedure: Connect an LED with a 330Ω resistor to Pin 13 (PB5) and configure it as an OUTPUT.
Connect a Push Button between Pin 2 (PD2) and Ground (GND).
Configure PD2 as an INPUT by clearing bit 2 in the DDRD register (DDRD &= ~(1<<PD2)).
Enable the internal pull-up resistor on PD2 by setting bit 2 in the PORTD register (PORTD |= (1<<PD2)).
In your main loop, read the PIND register to check the state of PD2.
If the bit is 0 (button pressed), turn the LED ON.
If the bit is 1 (button unpressed), turn the LED OFF.

posttest.question: When using an internal pull-up with a button connected to Ground, what does the PIN register read when the button is PRESSED?
posttest.explanation: Pressing the button connects the pin directly to Ground, forcing the voltage to 0V (LOW).

posttest.question: Why is it good practice to use #define for pin numbers?
posttest.explanation: #define creates named constants for self-documenting code.

posttest.question: What tool converts C source to .hex for AVR?
posttest.explanation: avr-gcc compiles C to AVR machine code.

posttest.question: What is the difference between PORTB and PINB?
options: PORTB writes output; PINB reads actual pin voltages
posttest.explanation: PORTB drives outputs; PINB reads real-time pin states.

posttest.question: What happens to SRAM when power is removed?
What is the stack used for in AVR?
posttest.explanation: Stack stores return addresses and local variables.

posttest.question: Which is NOT a valid ATmega328P port?
posttest.explanation: ATmega328P has only Port B, C, and D. No Port A.

posttest.question: What is a watchdog timer used for?
posttest.explanation: WDT auto-resets MCU when not periodically fed.

posttest.question: What does the SREG register contain?
posttest.explanation: SREG holds CPU flags and the global interrupt enable bit.

posttest.question: In an ISR, interrupts are automatically:
posttest.explanation: AVR clears I-flag in SREG upon entering an ISR.

feedback: Great job! You learned how to read inputs, use bitwise operators to check specific bits within PIN registers, and enable internal pull-up resistors to stabilize inputs.
